title: The Fence Principle

content.statement: Don't tear down a rule until you understand why someone built it.
content.explainer: Rules and constraints usually exist because they once solved a real problem you may no longer see. Removing one you don't understand isn't boldness — it's gambling that the original danger has vanished.
questions: Which rule or process are you itching to delete without knowing why it was put there?
Who created the constraint you're about to remove, and what were they protecting against?
What's the worst that could happen if the reason for this old rule still quietly holds?
sop: Identify the rule, process, or constraint you want to remove.
Trace who put it there and what problem they were solving — ask people, dig through history, don't guess.
Rate your understanding of its original purpose from 1 to 10 (see calculation, page 6).
If you score low, keep the rule in place and keep investigating until the reason is clear.
Remove it only once you understand it and can confirm the original risk is genuinely gone.

definitions.def: Don't take down a fence until you know why it was put up, because it may be quietly solving a problem you can't currently see.

content.equation: Removal Safety = Understanding of Its Original Purpose (rate 1-10)
content.equation_example: You rate your understanding of the rule's original purpose at 3 out of 10. 3 ÷ 10 = 0.3 — low removal safety, so leave it alone for now. When your understanding reaches 9 ÷ 10 = 0.9, you can remove it with real confidence.
content.closing_statement: Respecting what you don't yet understand is the humility that keeps you from breaking things still holding weight.
content.question: Which rule or process are you itching to delete without knowing why it was put there?

caption:
Principle Nº 340: The Fence Principle

Don't tear down a rule until you understand why someone built it.

Ask yourself: Which rule or process are you itching to delete without knowing why it was put there?
